perturbation.original_text: The Company shall ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, shall (i) be substantially the same as or greater than the quality of goods and services provided under such Seller Licensed Trademarks immediately prior to the Effective Date and (ii) not be associated with any goods or services, including any activities, that are reasonably likely to have an adverse effect on (A) the image or reputation of any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or (B) Seller's right, title or interest in and to, any of the Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall endeavor to ensure that all goods and services provided by the Company, under or in association with any of the Seller Licensed Trademarks, are of acceptable quality, and should avoid association with any goods or services that might have an adverse effect on the Seller Licensed Trademarks or Arizona Licensed Trademarks.
perturbation.explanation: The modification replaces 'shall ensure...shall be' with 'shall endeavor to ensure...are of acceptable quality', which is less strict and introduces subjectivity. 'Reasonably likely to have an adverse effect' is replaced with 'might have an adverse effect' and it weakens the original obligation.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act protects trademark owners from use of their trademarks in a way that is likely to cause confusion, or to deceive consumers. By changing the contract language to 'endeavor to ensure' and 'acceptable quality', the contract creates an ambiguous standard of quality control, which is not likely to protect against consumer deception or dilution of the trademark, contrary to the Lanham Act's purpose.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1114 - Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers U.S. Code Notes prev | next (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant— (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit , copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive; or (b) reproduce, counterfeit , copy, or colorably imitate a registered mark and apply such reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation to labels, signs, prints, packages, wrappers, receptacles or advertisements intended to be used in commerce upon or in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of goods or services on or in connection with which such use is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive, shall be liable in a civil action by the registrant for the remedies hereinafter provided. Under subsection (b) hereof, the registrant shall not be entitled to recover profits or damages unless the acts have been committed with knowledge that such imitation is intended to be used to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive. As used in this paragraph, the term “ any person ” includes the United States, all agencies and instrumentalities thereof, and all individuals, firms, corporations, or other persons acting for the United States and with the authorization and consent of the United States, and any State, any instrumentality of a State, and any officer or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Remedies; infringement; innocent infringement by printers and publishers (1) Any person who shall, without the consent of the registrant- (a) use in commerce any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark in connection with the sale, offering for sale, distribution, or advertising of any g
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the quality of goods and services provided under the licensed trademarks and weakens the obligation to avoid adverse effects on the trademarks, potentially conflicting with the Lanham Act's goal of protecting trademark owners from uses likely to cause confusion or deception. By changing 'shall ensure...shall be' to 'shall endeavor to ensure...are of acceptable quality,' the contract replaces a strict obligation with a subjective standard. The original text's 'reasonably likely to have an adverse effect' is replaced with 'might have an adverse effect', weakening the protection. According to 15 U.S. Code § 1114, trademark infringement occurs when someone uses 'any reproduction, counterfeit, copy, or colorable imitation of a registered mark' in a way that 'is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive.' The modified contract language could be interpreted as providing insufficient protection against such infringement because the 'acceptable quality' standard is open to interpretation and the endeavor to ensure may not be a strong enough safeguard. The changed version lowers the standard.

perturbation.original_text: The Company shall not tarnish or bring into disrepute the reputation of or goodwill associated with the Seller Licensed Trademarks or Arizona.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company should generally avoid actions that could potentially tarnish the reputation of the Seller Licensed Trademarks or Arizona.
perturbation.explanation: The language changes from 'shall not tarnish' to 'should generally avoid actions that could potentially tarnish'. The original text creates a strict obligation to prevent tarnishment. The revised text introduces a subjective element ('generally avoid') and weakens the commitment, making enforcement difficult.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 1125 - False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution forbidden
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 1125(c) of the Lanham Act prohibits trademark dilution. The original text provided strong protection against actions that could harm the trademark's reputation. The revised text weakens this protection, making it more difficult to prevent actions that dilute the brand, going against the intent of this section of the Lanham Act.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 15 U.S. Code § 1125 - False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution forbidden | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1125 - False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution forbidden U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Civil action (1) Any person who, on or in connection with any goods or services, or any container for goods, uses in commerce any word, term, name, symbol, or device, or any combination thereof, or any false designation of origin, false or misleading description of fact, or false or misleading representation of fact, which— (A) is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive as to the affiliation, connection, or association of such person with another person , or as to the origin, sponsorship, or approval of his or her goods, services, or commercial activities by another person , or (B) in commercial advertising or promotion, misrepresents the nature, characteristics, qualities, or geographic origin of his or her or another person ’s goods, services, or commercial activities, shall be liable in a civil action by any person who believes that he or she is or is likely to be damaged by such act. (2) As used in this subsection, the term “ any person ” includes any State, instrumentality of a State or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Any State, and any such instrumentality, officer, or employee, shall be subject to the provisions of this chapter in the same manner and to the same extent as any nongovernmental entity. (3) In a civil action for trade dress infringement under this chapter for trade dress not registered on the principal register, the person who asserts trade dress protection has the burden of proving that the matter sought to be protected is not functional. False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution forbidden (a) Civil action (1) Any person who, on or in connection with any goods or services, or any container for goods, uses in commerce any word, term, name, symbol, or device, or any combination thereof, or any false designation of origi
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the original obligation, creating an ambiguous standard where a clear prohibition previously existed. The original text states the Company 'shall not tarnish,' imposing a strict duty. The modified text says the company 'should generally avoid actions that could potentially tarnish,' introducing a subjective and weaker standard. This directly contradicts the Lanham Act's aim, as explained by the Law Explanation, to prevent actions that dilute a brand's reputation. While the snippets do not explicitly define the level of protection required, they establish the prohibition of 'dilution forbidden' in 15 U.S. Code § 1125. The change from 'shall not' to 'should generally avoid' introduces ambiguity, making enforcement more difficult and undermining the law's intent to protect against trademark dilution, thus creating a legal contradiction as an ambiguous legal obligation.

perturbation.original_text: The Company shall use the Seller Licensed Trademarks at all times in compliance with all applicable Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall attempt to use the Seller Licensed Trademarks in a manner that is generally consistent with applicable laws.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the firm 'shall use... in compliance with' to the weaker 'shall attempt to use... generally consistent with'. This weakens the obligation to adhere to applicable laws, making it ambiguous whether full compliance is required.
perturbation.law_citation: 18 U.S. Code § 2320 - Trafficking in counterfeit goods or services
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 2320 criminalizes trafficking in goods or services bearing counterfeit marks. The original text required full compliance with all applicable laws, ensuring alignment with this Act. The modified text's 'attempt to use...generally consistent' introduces ambiguity about the required level of adherence, potentially conflicting with the strict prohibitions of the Trademark Counterfeiting Act, which requires full compliance with trademark law to avoid liability.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 2320 - Trafficking in counterfeit goods or services | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 2320 - Trafficking in counterfeit goods or services U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Offenses.— Whoever intentionally— (1) traffics in goods or services and knowingly uses a counterfeit mark on or in connection with such goods or services, (2) traffics in labels, patches, stickers, wrappers, badges, emblems, medallions, charms, boxes, containers, cans, cases, hangtags, documentation, or packaging of any type or nature, knowing that a counterfeit mark has been applied thereto, the use of which is likely to cause confusion, to cause mistake, or to deceive, (3) traffics in goods or services knowing that such good or service is a counterfeit military good or service the use, malfunction, or failure of which is likely to cause serious bodily injury or death, the disclosure of classified information, impairment of combat operations, or other significant harm to a combat operation, a member of the Armed Forces, or to national security, or (4) traffics in a drug and knowingly uses a counterfeit mark on or in connection with such drug, or attempts or conspires to violate any of paragraphs (1) through (4) shall be punished as provided in subsection (b). (b) Penalties.— (1) In general.— Whoever commits an offense under subsection (a)— (A) if an individual, shall be fined not more than $2,000,000 or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both, and, if a person other than an individual, shall be fined not more than $5,000,000; and (B) for a second or subsequent offense under subsection (a), if an individual, shall be fined not more than $5,000,000 or imprisoned not more than 20 years, or both, and if other than an individual, shall be fined not more than $15,000,000. (2) Serious bodily injury or death.— (A) Serious bodily injury.— Whoever knowingly or recklessly causes or

Trafficking in counterfeit goods or services (a) Offenses .-Whoever intentionally- (1) traffics in goods or services and knowingly uses a counterfeit mark on or in connection with such goods or services, (2) traffics in labels, patches, stickers, wrappers, badges, emblems, medallions, charms, boxes, containers, cans, cases, hangtags, documentation, or packaging of any type
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to comply with applicable laws concerning trademarks, creating a potential contradiction. The original text mandates full compliance, while the modified text only requires an 'attempt to use...generally consistent,' introducing ambiguity. This ambiguity is problematic because, as 18 U.S. Code § 2320 states, anyone who 'traffics in goods or services and knowingly uses a counterfeit mark' is committing an offense. The original text prevents this, while the changed text opens the door to it, because it is ambiguous about what level of adherence is needed.
